projectIntentSummary: codex-fleet is a centralized capability gateway and control plane for Codex hosts. Its intent is to move capability discovery, policy enforcement, routing, and disclosure into a single server-side system exposed through MCP and admin APIs instead of relying on host-local skill repos, sync flows, or activation links. Skills are treated as source material that can be compiled into structured capability packages with progressive disclosure, so listings stay compact and full instructions are only revealed on explicit read or execution. The codebase is a TypeScript monorepo with separate apps for the MCP server, admin API, and web UI, plus shared packages for registry/schema, policy, telemetry, execution-provider abstractions, skill indexing, and first-class skill import. The latest README update confirms a major milestone: module_runtime is now documented as implemented for local JS/Python-style tool entrypoints over stdin/stdout JSON, while host_runner, mcp_proxy, and http_api still remain structured not-yet-implemented responses. The updated server, importer, and compiler files show the project is also tightening source disclosure, validating imported archives more defensively, and moving the package-based skill pipeline from design into concrete behavior.
objectivesSummary: The visible objectives are to: 1) serve as the single centralized MCP capability gateway for Codex hosts; 2) compile skills into canonical capability packages rather than leaving them as raw files; 3) preserve progressive disclosure so capability listings stay lightweight and details are fetched only when needed; 4) support a registry that can represent tools, resources, prompts, modules, and connectors; 5) enforce default-deny access with signed tokens, ACL/scoped checks, package allow/deny rules, revocation, and audit logging; 6) keep MCP as the primary agent-facing protocol while using REST/web surfaces for admin and control-plane tasks; 7) preserve execution-provider extensibility so different backends can be added without hardcoding provider-specific behavior; and 8) make skill import first-class, including markdown and archive-based inputs, without reintroducing repo-sync or file-replication semantics. The current diffs emphasize objectives 2, 3, and 8: the compiler now derives package identity and metadata from frontmatter, manifest, and package.json inputs; the importer now validates archives and inspects skill metadata more carefully; and the MCP server now only reads source-backed resources from allowed package roots. The README update also shows objective 7 is partially realized via module_runtime, while the remaining provider variants are still pending.
constraintsSummary: Several constraints shape the architecture. The project is a Node.js/TypeScript monorepo using npm workspaces and Turborepo-style organization, with a pinned package-manager setup. Persistent server state is expected to live locally under .capabilities-style directories, with separate storage for capability packages and policy data, and LOCAL_REPO_PATH defaults to the current working directory. Security is explicitly default-deny: capabilities must satisfy scope checks and policy rules before they are listed or executed, and auditability is part of the model. MCP is the primary consumption surface for agents; REST is intended for admin/control-plane work. Another strong constraint is negative: repo cloning, host-local skill-tree synchronization, activation flows, and file-replication-based capability delivery are explicitly outside the target architecture. The new import and execution support must remain compatible with existing markdown-based skills during migration, and execution-provider extensibility must be preserved rather than replaced with ad hoc logic. The server changes add an additional filesystem boundary: source-backed resources may only be read from allowed package roots, not arbitrary paths. The README also makes clear that only module_runtime is implemented today, while host_runner, mcp_proxy, and http_api still need work.
principlesGuidelinesSummary: No standalone principles, guidelines, conventions, or contributor-guide file was found in repository exploration. A search for common names such as CONTRIBUTING, GUIDELINES, CONVENTIONS, STYLE, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, and PRINCIPLES returned no matches. However, the repository’s operating principles are clearly embedded in README.md and the planning docs. The recurring themes are: centralize discovery and policy instead of distributing skills across hosts; use progressive disclosure so packages stay compact until explicitly expanded; keep access default-deny and authorize by scope, tags, and package rules; make capability packages the canonical unit of management rather than raw markdown files; preserve transitional compatibility with SKILL.md and other markdown sources while introducing structured manifests/frontmatter; keep MCP as the public agent-facing protocol while reserving REST as an admin/control-plane API; and use standard contracts for execution-provider backends instead of provider-specific one-offs. The current code changes reinforce those principles by adding frontmatter-aware compilation, tighter source-root checking, archive safety checks, and more explicit package-backed handling of imported skill material. In practical terms, the repo’s conventions appear to favor explicit metadata, bounded disclosure, local filesystem trust boundaries, and migration-friendly evolution over sudden format replacement.
implementationStatusSummary: The core gateway foundation is already in place: the repository has a capability registry, policy engine, MCP server, API, and web UI. The latest changes look like implementation hardening and feature completion rather than greenfield scaffolding. README.md now states that module_runtime is implemented for local JS/Python-style tool entrypoints, while host_runner, mcp_proxy, and http_api still remain unimplemented. In apps/mcp-server/src/server.ts, capability resources are being restricted to allowed source roots, which suggests tighter disclosure and safer source-ref handling. In packages/skill-importer/src/live.ts, archive handling is being made more defensive with traversal, symlink, duplicate-entry, and size checks, plus skill metadata inspection and warning generation. In packages/skill-indexer/src/compiler.ts, frontmatter, manifest, and package.json inputs are being merged into a richer compiled package model with summary resources, instructions resources, and an apply prompt. Overall, the project is past the planning phase for import and local module execution, but a few execution providers and some end-to-end wiring still remain incomplete.
topObjectives: Serve as the single centralized MCP capability gateway for Codex hosts
Compile skills into structured capability packages with progressive disclosure
Enforce default-deny access with scoped authorization and audit logging
Support multiple execution backends through pluggable provider interfaces
Make skill import first-class for markdown and archive sources
Expose MCP as the primary agent-facing surface and REST/web as admin surfaces
constraints: TypeScript monorepo using npm workspaces and Turborepo conventions
Persistent server state lives in local .capabilities-style directories
Default-deny access model for capability discovery and execution
MCP is the primary agent-facing interface; REST is admin/internal
No repo-sync, activation, or file-replication architecture in the target model
Import support must remain compatible with existing markdown-based skill inputs during migration
Source references must remain within allowed package roots

keyFiles.whyImportant: Primary vision and current-state document; explains the centralized capability-gateway model, progressive disclosure, the module_runtime milestone, and the move away from distributed sync/activation behavior.
Main architecture plan covering registry, compiler, MCP gateway, policy, execution providers, and migration strategy.
Focused architecture plan for first-class skill import, including archive/markdown intake and package persistence behavior.
Primary MCP gateway server entrypoint and a key integration point for registry, indexing, resource disclosure, and protocol surfaces; the latest change tightens source-root authorization.
Core importer implementation for markdown, path, and archive sources; important for understanding the new package-based ingestion pipeline and archive safety checks.
Compiler logic for converting skills into canonical capability artifacts; central to the frontmatter-aware pipeline and package generation changes.

vision.rationale: README.md explicitly states the project’s intent: a centralized capability gateway for Codex hosts, with discovery and policy centralized and local execution delegated only when necessary. It also documents the shift from generic scaffolding to a working module_runtime execution path and the removal of repo-sync/activation behavior from the main product surface.

architecture.rationale: PLAN.md and PLAN-skills-package-import.md provide the clearest architecture evidence, and the updated importer/compiler/server files show the plan is being implemented. Together they describe the registry schema, skill compilation pipeline, MCP gateway, policy model, and the new import flow. The updated live importer file also shows archive intake, inspection, and validation logic becoming real.

principles_guidelines.rationale: No dedicated principles, conventions, or contributor-guide file was found in repository exploration. A targeted search for common document names returned no matches, so the guiding ideas appear to be embedded in README.md and the planning docs rather than captured as a standalone repository conventions document.

openQuestions: Will host_runner, mcp_proxy, and http_api gain implementations aligned with the new module_runtime path, or remain intentionally unimplemented for now?
How are allowed source roots configured and persisted, and should they be user- or package-defined?
What is the remaining end-to-end wiring needed so importer/compiler changes fully land in registry and disclosure behavior?
Do the new source-root restrictions apply only to resources, or also to execution and import provenance?
reseedNotes: This reseed is driven by key file changes in README.md, apps/mcp-server/src/server.ts, packages/skill-importer/src/live.ts, and packages/skill-indexer/src/compiler.ts. The important deltas are: module_runtime is now documented as implemented, the MCP server is tightening source-ref access through allowed source roots, the importer is hardening archive ingestion and metadata inspection, and the compiler is producing richer package-backed capability artifacts from frontmatter/manifest/package.json inputs. Future work should focus on the new local tool execution path, the security implications of source-root allowlisting, and any remaining integration gaps between import, compilation, registry storage, and discovery.
